function.name: web_search
function.description: Search the public internet for information. Each result will contain a url, a title, and one excerpt taken directly from the page.

start_date.description: (optional) The start date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. Must not be equal to end_date.

end_date.description: (optional) The end date for the search range in the format YYYY-MM-DD. If the value is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

number_of_results.description: (optional) The number of search results to return.

function.name: retrieve_information
function.description: Retrieves critical data segments from a previously ingested document via hybrid lexical and semantic calculations. Crucial Requirement: The search prompt argument MUST contain the token placeholder containing your storage key inside double curly brackets, exactly format formatted as: {{key_name}} alongside your query description text.

prompt.description: Your target question containing the explicit target reference mapping (e.g., 'What were the total research costs in {{my_document_key}}?').

preserve_document_order.description: Set to True if processing complex structural data dependencies across sequential disclosures.

function.name: parse_html_page
function.description: Parses an HTML document or SEC filing. It automatically extracts key metadata, converts asset tables to clean markdown, slices sections into optimal word chunks, and runs concurrent contextual LLM enrichment before updating the disk cache.

url.description: The target URL of the HTML page/filing to process.

key.description: The unique cache storage key for saving and loading the index.

function.name: edgar_search
function.description: Search the EDGAR Database through the SEC API. You should provide a search query. You can also optionally provide a start date, an end date, a page number, top N results, a list of form types, and/or a list of CIKs. The results are returned as a list of dictionaries, each containing the metadata for a filing. It does not contain the full text of the filing.

search_query.description: The case-insensitive search-term or phrase to search the contents of filings and their attachments. This can be a single word, phrase, or combination of words and phrases. Supported search features include wildcards (*), Boolean operators (OR, NOT), and exact phrase matching by enclosing phrases in quotation marks ("exact phrase"). By default, all terms are joined by an implicit AND operator.

form_types.description: (optional) Limits search to specific EDGAR form types (e.g., ['8-K', '10-Q']) list of strings. Default: all form types

ciks.description: (optional) Filters results to filings from specified CIKs, type list of strings. Leading zeros are optional but may be included. Example: [ "0001811414", "1318605" ]. Default: all CIKs

start_date.description: (optional) Start date for the search range in yyyy-mm-dd format. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

end_date.description: (optional) End date for the search range, in the same format as startDate. If the value is a date that is later than 2026-03-01, it will be set to 2026-03-01.

page.description: (optional) Used for pagination. Each page contains up to 100 matching filings. Increase the page number to retrieve the next set of 100 filings. Example: 3 retrieves the third page. Default: 1

top_n_results.description: (optional) Return only the first N results out of 100 from the page. If not provided, all 100 results will be returned. E.g. if page is 2, and top_n_results is 10, you will receive results 100 to 110.

function.name: calculator
function.description: Evaluate a mathematical expression and return the result. Use this tool for all arithmetic calculations instead of computing by hand. Supports: +, -, *, /, ** (exponentiation), % (modulo), and parentheses for grouping. Available functions: abs(), min(), max(), sqrt(), log(), log10(). Examples: '(5000 - 3200) * 0.21', '(2865507 / 1905871) ** 0.5 - 1', '14060 / 2148'.

function.name: price_history
function.description: Fetch historical daily price data for a specific asset class. Returns a CSV table with one row per day. Use asset_class='equity' or 'etf' for US-listed stocks/ETFs (e.g. AAPL, SPY), 'crypto' for pairs like btcusd (lowercase, no dash), or 'fx' for pairs like audusd. Non-US equities and most indices/futures are not covered by the pricing provider. Each row includes raw OHLC (open/high/low/close), split- and dividend-adjusted OHLC (adjOpen/adjHigh/adjLow/adjClose), raw and adjusted volume, divCash (dividend amount on the date), and splitFactor.

ticker.description: Ticker symbol. High-level symbology rules: (1) Equity/ETF/mutual fund: use the US exchange-listed symbol (AAPL, SPY, VFIAX). Symbols are case-insensitive and contain only letters and dashes. (2) Share classes use a dash, not a dot: 'BRK-A' / 'BRK-B' (not 'BRK.A'), 'BF-B'. (3) Securities are identified by the ticker as listed on the primary US exchange; non-US listings, OTC pink sheets, indices, and futures are generally not covered. (4) Tickers can be reused after delisting; data is point-in-time for the entity that held the ticker on each date. (5) Crypto: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'btcusd', 'ethusd'. (6) FX: concatenated lowercase pair with no separator, e.g. 'audusd', 'eurusd'.

asset_class.description: Asset class used to route to the proper pricing endpoint.

function.name: submit_final_result
function.description: Submits the final answer to the user. You should include your final answer, as well as any necessary reasoning, justification, calculations, and explanation. Finally, you should provide any sources used to answer the question. You MUST use this tool to submit your final result. The user will not see your response if you do not use this tool to submit. You will not be able to continue working after this tool is called; the conversation will be ended.

final_result.description: The final result to submit to the user

steps.message:
You are an IPO and financial diligence agent.

You are given a question and must answer it using the tools provided.

You cannot interact with the user or ask clarifying questions.

Answer solely based on available information and tools.

Assume the current date is May 31, 2026.

---

TOOLS AND DATA SOURCES

You have access to a document storage system.

You may store parsed HTML retrieved from the web and later retrieve
information using LLM-based retrieval prompts.

Use this system to avoid context-window limitations.

When working with long filings, prefer section-specific retrieval prompts
rather than broad document-level retrieval.

Examples:

- retrieve the Segment Adjusted EBITDA reconciliation table from MD&A
- retrieve the glossary definition of Starshield
- retrieve the controlled-company governance disclosure

For peer-comparison questions:

- retrieve peer SEC filings using edgar_search
- do not rely on memory for peer financial figures

For historical public-equity price data not available in SEC filings:

- use price_history as primary source
- use raw unadjusted prices unless adjusted prices are requested
- fall back to web_search only if price_history fails

Stock indices are not covered by price_history.

Use authoritative sources such as FRED or web_search for index levels.

---

SOURCE HIERARCHY

Prioritize sources in this order:

1. S-1 / S-1A filings
2. Incorporated SEC filings
3. SEC exhibits and merger documents
4. SEC periodic filings
5. Company earnings materials
6. External sources

If figures differ across sources:

prefer the most authoritative SEC source containing the directly reported
figure and explain material discrepancies when relevant.

SEC filings are the primary and most authoritative source of financial and
disclosure information.

---

ANSWER STANDARDS

You must call submit_final_result with the completed answer.

Your submission will not be processed unless submit_final_result is called.

Include:

- complete reasoning
- calculations
- supporting analysis

You will be evaluated on:

- final-answer accuracy
- analytical correctness

Do not round intermediate calculations.

Round only final reported values.

When possible, report calculated figures to at least two decimal places.

Report financial figures using the same scale and units used in the filing
unless otherwise specified.

Share prices should be reported with two decimal places.

---

DISCLOSURE DISCIPLINE

Before reaching a conclusion:

determine whether the filing provides sufficient information to answer:

- directly
- partially
- only through inference

Distinguish carefully among:

- facts explicitly disclosed
- reasonable filing-supported inferences
- information not disclosed

Do not speculate beyond what the filing supports.

If material information is absent:

explicitly state that the filing does not provide sufficient information to
conclude.

Absence of disclosure may itself be analytically material.

---

FORWARD-LOOKING STATEMENTS

Treat separately:

- historical facts
- roadmap targets
- commercialization expectations
- management intentions
- forward-looking statements

Do not present aspirations or plans as historical outcomes.

---

GOVERNANCE AND LEGAL QUESTIONS

For governance, legal, or disclosure-language questions:

use the filing's precise terminology whenever possible.

Distinguish among:

- requirements
- exemptions
- company elections
- forward-looking language

---

ACCOUNTING POLICY QUESTIONS

For accounting-policy questions:

1. Identify the relevant disclosed accounting policy or treatment
2. Determine whether the filing explicitly resolves the issue
3. If unresolved, state that the filing does not specify treatment

---

NON-GAAP QUESTIONS

For non-GAAP measures:

identify:

- the starting GAAP metric
- each disclosed adjustment
- whether economically recurring costs are excluded

Do not accept non-GAAP measures without analyzing their construction.

---

AUDIENCE TAGS

Questions may include audience tags such as:

[PUBLIC | BANK]
[VC | CREDIT]
[LEGAL | ACCOUNTING]

Use these tags to calibrate terminology and analytical emphasis while
remaining grounded in disclosed facts.

---

SOURCES

At the end of your answer provide:

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "...",
      "name": "..."
    }
  ]
}


---


FILING CONTEXT

The primary filing for this benchmark is the SpaceX S-1 filed May 20, 2026:

https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm

Important:

The consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under
common-control accounting to include:

- xAI (merged February 2, 2026)
- X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025)

for all historical periods presented.

No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively.

When interpreting historical segment figures, state whether they reflect the
recast consolidated entity.

SpaceX (SPCX) has no public trading history as of May 30, 2026.

Do not attempt price_history retrieval for SpaceX or SPCX.

For peer valuation or peer financial benchmarking, retrieve peer-company SEC
filings using edgar_search rather than relying on memory.

Question:
Connectivity revenue represented what percentage of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue? Separately, how many quarters of current Starship R&D spending would equal one year of FY2025 Connectivity capex? Which metric better captures cross-segment dependency?

steps.reasoning_content:
The user is asking about SpaceX's S-1 filing. I need to find:
1. Connectivity revenue as a percentage of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue
2. How many quarters of current Starship R&D spending would equal one year of FY2025 Connectivity capex
3. Which metric better captures cross-segment dependency

Let me start by parsing the SpaceX S-1 filing.

Find Starship R&D spending or research and development expenses related to Starship program for Q1 2026 and FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}
Find Connectivity segment capital expenditures (capex) for FY2025 in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:

* **Connectivity Segment Revenue:** $3,257 million
* **Total Consolidated Revenue:** $4,694 million

This data is confirmed in both the revenue disaggregation table (which notes that all $380 million in products revenue is attributable to the Connectivity segment) and the reportable segment financial table.

Based on the provided text, the research and development (R&D) expenses related to the Starship program are as follows:

*   **Q1 2026 (Three months ended March 31, 2026):** $930 million
*   **FY2025 (Year ended December 31, 2025):** $3,004 million 

The document specifies that the Space segment funded these amounts in research and development expense for the next-generation Starship launch vehicle program during these respective periods.
Based on the provided financial overview, the Connectivity segment's capital expenditures (capex) for FY2025 were **$4,178 million**.

steps.message: Let me verify these figures by retrieving more specific information from the filing.
steps.reasoning_content:
Let me verify these numbers by retrieving more specific information. I want to make sure I have the right figures.

Let me calculate:
1. Connectivity revenue as % of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue = $3,257M / $4,694M
2. FY2025 Connectivity capex = $4,178M
3. Q1 2026 Starship R&D = $930M
4. Quarters of Starship R&D = $4,178M / $930M

Let me verify these numbers more carefully.

Find the segment capital expenditures table showing capex by segment (Launch, Space, Connectivity) for FY2025 and Q1 2026 in {{spacex_s1}}
Find the Starship R&D expense disclosure and any narrative about Starship development spending in {{spacex_s1}}

results.content:
Here is the reportable segment financial information table showing revenue disaggregated by type and segment for the three months ended March 31, 2026 and the year ended December 31, 2025:

| Revenue Disaggregated by Type and Segment | Three Months Ended March 31, 2026 | Year Ended December 31, 2025 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Launch Services | $330 | $2,576 |
| Launch & Development | 289 | 1,510 |
| **Space** | **619** | **4,086** |
| Consumer | 2,148 | 7,208 |
| Enterprise & Government (1) | 1,109 | 4,179 |
| **Connectivity** | **3,257** | **11,387** |
| Advertising | 343 | 1,844 |
| AI Solutions & Infrastructure | 475 | 1,357 |
| **AI** | **818** | **3,201** |
| **Total revenues** | **$4,694** | **$18,674** |

Based on the provided text, here is the segment capital expenditures table showing capex by segment for FY2025 and Q1 2026, along with the explanation of the year-over-year increases in the Space segment:

**The following table presents our capital expenditures by segment:**

| (in millions) | Three Months Ended March 31, 2026 | Three Months Ended March 31, 2025 | Year Ended December 31, 2025 | Year Ended December 31, 2024 | Year Ended December 31, 2023 |
| :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- | :--- |
| Space | $1,052 | $759 | $3,832 | $2,032 | $1,497 |
| Connectivity | 1,332 | 814 | 4,178 | 3,498 | 2,455 |
| AI | 7,723 | 2,567 | 12,727 | 5,633 | 463 |
| **Total Capital Expenditures** | **$10,107** | **$4,140** | **$20,737** | **$11,163** | **$4,415** |

**Space**
Space capital expenditures for the three months ended March 31, 2026 increased $293 million to $1,052 million compared to $759 million for the three months ended March 31, 2025. The increase was primarily driven by increased investment in our launch site infrastructure for Starship.

Space capital expenditures for 2025 increased $1,800 million to $3,832 million compared to $2,032 million in 2024, while Space capital expenditures for 2024 increased $535 million to $2,032 million compared to $1,497 million in 2023. The increase in each year-over-year period was primarily driven by increased investment in our launch site infrastructure for Starship.

Based on the provided financial disclosures, here is the information regarding Starship R&D expenses and the narrative surrounding its development spending:

### Starship R&D Expense Disclosures
*   **Q1 2026:** The Space segment funded **$930 million** in research and development expense for the Starship program. 
*   **Full Year 2025:** The Space segment funded **$3,004 million** (an increase from $1,835 million in 2024) in R&D expense for Starship.
*   **Q1 2026 R&D Increase:** Space segment R&D increased by **$404 million** (76.8%) compared to Q1 2025. This was driven by higher production costs ($194 million), higher engineering costs ($95 million), and higher test and launch costs ($62 million) due to accelerated investment in Starship.
*   **Full Year 2025 R&D Increase:** Space segment R&D increased by **$1,169 million** (63.7%) compared to 2024. This was driven by higher production costs ($779 million), higher launch costs ($218 million), and higher engineering costs ($185 million).
*   **Full Year 2024 R&D Increase:** Space segment R&D increased by **$297 million** (19.3%) compared to 2023 due to higher production, launch, and engineering costs for Starship and related facilities.

### Narrative on Starship Development Spending
**Purpose and Strategic Vision:** 
Starship is designed to enable a step-function change in launch capability across reusability, payload capacity, and launch cadence. It is described as the "key enabler of our long-term growth strategy" that will unlock entirely new categories of missions. The company expects Starship V3 to carry 100 metric tons to orbit, with future generations reaching 200 metric tons. Ultimately, the company believes Starship can reduce the cost to reach orbit by 99% or more compared to the historical NASA average of $18,500 per kilogram, paving the way for future infrastructure like orbital AI compute.

**Composition of R&D Costs:**
Space segment R&D expenses mainly relate to the development, build, and testing of Starship. These costs consist of:
*   Test flight hardware and Raptor engines (which are expensed when used in test flights).
*   Employee compensation costs (salaries, benefits, share-based compensation).
*   Tooling and equipment expenses.
*   Depreciation for R&D equipment.
*   Allocated overhead.

**Accounting Treatment and Future Expectations:**
Because Starship is currently in the development stage, a majority of its costs are expensed to Research and Development as incurred. The company expects Space R&D to continue increasing in absolute dollars and as a percentage of revenue throughout 2026 as development and commercialization efforts accelerate. 

Once Starship is commercialized (defined as delivering payload to orbit), the company expects R&D spending to moderate. At the point of commercialization, Starship costs will generally shift from being expensed as R&D to being capitalized and depreciated in the cost of revenue of the segment associated with the payload delivered.

3. Which metric better captures cross-segment dependency?
   - The first metric (Connectivity revenue as % of consolidated revenue) shows how much of the consolidated revenue comes from Connectivity

The Starship program is being developed to support other segments (Connectivity, AI). The fact that ~4.5 quarters of Starship R&D equals one year of Connectivity capex shows the relative scale of investment in the enabling technology vs the dependent segment's infrastructure.

Actually, let me think about this more carefully. The question is about "cross-segment dependency." 

The first metric (Connectivity revenue as % of consolidated revenue) shows the revenue concentration in Connectivity - this is a measure of how dependent the consolidated entity is on Connectivity revenue. This is a within-segment metric.

The second metric (Starship R&D vs Connectivity capex) shows the relationship between investment in Starship (Space segment) and Connectivity capex. Starship is described as enabling future growth across segments. This shows the cross-segment dependency because Starship (Space segment) is being developed to support other segments including Connectivity (e.g., launching Starlink satellites more cheaply).

Actually, the cross-segment dependency is better captured by the second metric because:
- Starship is in the Space segment but is being developed to enable growth in other segments (Connectivity, AI)
- The fact that Starship R&D spending is comparable in scale to Connectivity capex shows the interdependency between segments
- The first metric just shows revenue concentration, not cross-segment dependency

steps.reasoning_content:
Let me also verify the data by looking at the segment table once more to make sure I have the right numbers.

Connectivity Q1 2026 revenue: $3,257M
Total Q1 2026 revenue: $4,694M
Percentage: 69.39%

FY2025 Connectivity capex: $4,178M
Q1 2026 Starship R&D: $930M
Quarters: 4.49 quarters

Now for the cross-segment dependency question:

The first metric (Connectivity revenue as % of consolidated revenue) shows revenue concentration - how much of consolidated revenue comes from one segment. This is a measure of revenue diversification/concentration, not cross-segment dependency.

The second metric (Starship R&D vs Connectivity capex) shows the relationship between investment in Starship (Space segment) and Connectivity capex. Starship is described as the "key enabler of our long-term growth strategy" that will unlock new categories of missions including orbital AI compute. The Starship program is being developed to support other segments (Connectivity, AI). This shows cross-segment dependency because:
- Starship (Space segment) is being developed to enable growth in other segments
- The relative scale of investment shows the interdependency between segments
- The first metric just shows revenue concentration, not cross-segment dependency

So the second metric better captures cross-segment dependency.

Let me submit my final answer.

arguments.final_result:
## Answer

### 1. Connectivity Revenue as % of Consolidated Q1 2026 Revenue

From the SpaceX S-1 reportable segment financial information table (three months ended March 31, 2026):

| Segment | Q1 2026 Revenue ($M) |
|---|---|
| Space | 619 |
| **Connectivity** | **3,257** |
| AI | 818 |
| **Total consolidated revenue** | **4,694** |

**Calculation:** $3,257M ÷ $4,694M = **69.39%**

Connectivity revenue represented approximately **69.39%** of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue.

---

### 2. Quarters of Starship R&D Spending Equal to One Year of FY2025 Connectivity Capex

From the S-1 disclosures:

- **FY2025 Connectivity segment capital expenditures:** $4,178M
- **Q1 2026 Starship R&D expense (funded by the Space segment):** $930M

**Calculation:** $4,178M ÷ $930M = **4.49 quarters**

Approximately **4.49 quarters** (roughly 13.5 months) of current Starship R&D spending would equal one year of FY2025 Connectivity capex. In other words, Starship R&D is currently running at a quarterly pace (~$930M) that is only modestly below the entire annual Connectivity capex base (~$4,178M).

---

### 3. Which Metric Better Captures Cross-Segment Dependency?

**The second metric (Starship R&D vs. Connectivity capex) better captures cross-segment dependency.**

Reasoning:

- **The first metric (Connectivity revenue share of consolidated revenue)** measures *revenue concentration* — i.e., how much consolidated revenue depends on a single segment. It is a within-segment / portfolio-concentration metric, not a cross-segment dependency metric. It tells you that Connectivity is the dominant revenue contributor, but it says nothing about how segments interact with or depend on one another.

- **The second metric (Starship R&D vs. Connectivity capex)** directly links investment in one segment (Space — Starship development) to the capital base of another segment (Connectivity). The S-1 explicitly describes Starship as the "key enabler of our long-term growth strategy" that will unlock new mission categories and reduce the cost to reach orbit by 99% or more — including enabling cheaper deployment of Starlink satellites (Connectivity) and future orbital AI compute (AI segment). The fact that ~4.5 quarters of Starship R&D equals an entire year of Connectivity capex quantifies the *relative scale of investment in the enabling technology (Starship) versus the dependent segment's infrastructure (Starlink ground/space assets)*. This is a genuine cross-segment dependency signal: the Space segment's R&D outlays are, in effect, subsidizing the long-term economics of the Connectivity (and AI) segments.

In short, the revenue-share metric captures *consolidation concentration*, while the Starship-R&D-to-Connectivity-capex ratio captures *cross-segment investment dependency* — the latter being the more analytically relevant measure of how segments depend on one another.

---

### Important Context

Per the filing, the consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under common-control accounting to include xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) for all historical periods presented. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively. The Q1 2026 figures above reflect the recast consolidated entity (Space + Connectivity + AI segments).

---

{
  "sources": [
    {
      "url": "https://www.sec.gov/Archives/edgar/data/1181412/000162828026036936/spaceexplorationtechnologi.htm",
      "name": "SpaceX S-1 Registration Statement (filed May 20, 2026) — Reportable Segment Financial Information; Capital Expenditures by Segment; Starship R&D disclosures"
    }
  ]
}

## Answer

### 1. Connectivity Revenue as % of Consolidated Q1 2026 Revenue

From the SpaceX S-1 reportable segment financial information table (three months ended March 31, 2026):

| Segment | Q1 2026 Revenue ($M) |
|---|---|
| Space | 619 |
| **Connectivity** | **3,257** |
| AI | 818 |
| **Total consolidated revenue** | **4,694** |

**Calculation:** $3,257M ÷ $4,694M = **69.39%**

Connectivity revenue represented approximately **69.39%** of consolidated Q1 2026 revenue.

---

### 2. Quarters of Starship R&D Spending Equal to One Year of FY2025 Connectivity Capex

From the S-1 disclosures:

- **FY2025 Connectivity segment capital expenditures:** $4,178M
- **Q1 2026 Starship R&D expense (funded by the Space segment):** $930M

**Calculation:** $4,178M ÷ $930M = **4.49 quarters**

Approximately **4.49 quarters** (roughly 13.5 months) of current Starship R&D spending would equal one year of FY2025 Connectivity capex. In other words, Starship R&D is currently running at a quarterly pace (~$930M) that is only modestly below the entire annual Connectivity capex base (~$4,178M).

---

### 3. Which Metric Better Captures Cross-Segment Dependency?

**The second metric (Starship R&D vs. Connectivity capex) better captures cross-segment dependency.**

Reasoning:

- **The first metric (Connectivity revenue share of consolidated revenue)** measures *revenue concentration* — i.e., how much consolidated revenue depends on a single segment. It is a within-segment / portfolio-concentration metric, not a cross-segment dependency metric. It tells you that Connectivity is the dominant revenue contributor, but it says nothing about how segments interact with or depend on one another.

- **The second metric (Starship R&D vs. Connectivity capex)** directly links investment in one segment (Space — Starship development) to the capital base of another segment (Connectivity). The S-1 explicitly describes Starship as the "key enabler of our long-term growth strategy" that will unlock new mission categories and reduce the cost to reach orbit by 99% or more — including enabling cheaper deployment of Starlink satellites (Connectivity) and future orbital AI compute (AI segment). The fact that ~4.5 quarters of Starship R&D equals an entire year of Connectivity capex quantifies the *relative scale of investment in the enabling technology (Starship) versus the dependent segment's infrastructure (Starlink ground/space assets)*. This is a genuine cross-segment dependency signal: the Space segment's R&D outlays are, in effect, subsidizing the long-term economics of the Connectivity (and AI) segments.

In short, the revenue-share metric captures *consolidation concentration*, while the Starship-R&D-to-Connectivity-capex ratio captures *cross-segment investment dependency* — the latter being the more analytically relevant measure of how segments depend on one another.

---

### Important Context

Per the filing, the consolidated financial statements have been retrospectively recast under common-control accounting to include xAI (merged February 2, 2026) and X Holdings (merged into xAI March 28, 2025) for all historical periods presented. No period reflects standalone SpaceX operations exclusively. The Q1 2026 figures above reflect the recast consolidated entity (Space + Connectivity + AI segments).
